backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The ACL runs from the anterior intercondylar area of the tibia to the lateral femoral condyle. It prevents anterior translation of the tibia.

Clinical Pearl: ACL tears are the most common sports ligament injury. Test with Lachman test (more sensitive than anterior drawer).

Memory Aid: ACL = Anterior Checks Laxity.

The medial meniscus is C-shaped and firmly attached to the MCL. It deepens the tibial plateau.

Clinical Pearl: More commonly torn — especially in the "unhappy triad" (ACL + MCL + medial meniscus) from a lateral blow to the knee.

Memory Aid: Medial Meniscus = Married to MCL.

The lateral meniscus is O-shaped and more mobile — not attached to the LCL.

Clinical Pearl: Its mobility means it is less frequently torn than the medial meniscus.

Memory Aid: Lateral = Loose — loosely attached, moves freely.

The patella is the largest sesamoid bone, embedded in the quadriceps tendon. It increases the mechanical advantage of the quadriceps by up to 50%.

Clinical Pearl: Patellar dislocation typically occurs laterally due to the Q-angle.

Memory Aid: Patella = Pulley for the quadriceps mechanism.

The LCL runs from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the head of the fibula. It resists varus stress.

Clinical Pearl: Unlike the MCL, the LCL is separate from the joint capsule and lateral meniscus. Test with varus stress test at 30° flexion.

Memory Aid: LCL = Lateral Cord to fibuLa.
